entries.title: Backup of the DPAPI Master Key was attempted
entries.summary: An attempt was made to back up the DPAPI Master Key.
entries.details: Generated when a DPAPI Master Key backup is attempted. DPAPI (Data Protection API) is used by Windows to encrypt sensitive data including saved browser credentials, Wi-Fi passwords, RDP saved credentials, and application secrets. Each user has a set of DPAPI Master Keys stored in their profile (AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Protect\<SID>). Key fields: SubjectAccountName (who initiated the backup), SubjectLogonId (session), KeyIdentifier (GUID of the master key being backed up), BackupType (0=recovery agent backup, 1=domain backup via DC). Legitimate backups occur automatically when a new master key is created or when the domain controller performs a backup via DPAPI RPC. Adversarial use: `mimikatz dpapi::masterkey /rpc` forces a backup request to the DC and extracts the plaintext master key — this generates a 4692 event. SubjectAccountName backing up keys for a different user's SID (lateral path), or backup activity from unexpected processes/hosts, is suspicious.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit DPAPI Activity' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > Object Access. Disabled by default.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Credentials from Password Stores: Credentials from Web Browsers

investigationPivots: SubjectAccountName performing a backup for a KeyIdentifier belonging to a different user's profile (different SID path) indicates cross-user credential theft
Backup activity originating from an unusual process (check parent process via EID 4688) — legitimate DPAPI backups come from lsass.exe or the CryptProtectData API, not cmd.exe or PowerShell
Correlate with network connections (Sysmon EID 3) from the same host to a DC on port 135/RPC at the same time — mimikatz dpapi::masterkey /rpc initiates an RPC call to the DC
KeyIdentifier can be cross-referenced with the master key files in the user's Protect folder to identify which credentials are at risk
commonFalsePositives: Automatic domain backup of newly created master keys by the Windows DPAPI infrastructure — occurs periodically and is normal
Backup during account migration or profile restoration by authorized IT staff

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account performing the master key backup; SYSTEM is normal for AD-backed backup, user accounts are suspicious

keyFields.name: MasterKeyId
keyFields.description: GUID of the DPAPI master key being backed up; correlate across 4692/4693 to track key lifecycle

keyFields.name: RecoveryServer
keyFields.description: DC used for the backup operation; backup to an unexpected DC or non-DC may indicate Mimikatz dpapi::masterkey /rpc

keyFields.name: ReturnCode
keyFields.description: 0x0 = success; non-zero indicates backup failure which may itself be suspicious

detectionRules.title: DPAPI Master Key Backup by Non-SYSTEM Account
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4692
| where SubjectUserName != "SYSTEM"
| project TimeGenerated, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, MasterKeyId, RecoveryServer, ReturnCode, Computer
detectionRules.notes: SYSTEM performs legitimate AD-backed DPAPI master key backups. Any user account performing this operation is abnormal and may indicate Mimikatz 'dpapi::masterkey /rpc' usage.

detectionRules.title: DPAPI Master Key Backup by Non-System Account
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4692 NOT Account_Name=SYSTEM
| table _time, Account_Name, Master_Key_ID, Recovery_Server, Return_Code, host
detectionRules.notes: Alert on user-context DPAPI master key backup operations.

detectionRules.title: DPAPI Master Key Backup by Non-System Account
detectionRules.rule:
title: DPAPI Master Key Backup by Non-System Account
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4692
    filter_system:
        SubjectUserName: 'SYSTEM'
    condition: selection and not filter_system
falsepositives:
    - Domain administrator performing manual key backup
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Low false positive rate; legitimate non-SYSTEM backups are rare. Investigate immediately.

entries.title: Recovery of the DPAPI Master Key was attempted
entries.summary: An attempt was made to recover the DPAPI Master Key.
entries.details: Generated when a DPAPI Master Key recovery is attempted. Recovery occurs when the local master key copy is unavailable (e.g., profile corruption, password reset) and the backup must be retrieved from the domain controller. Key fields: SubjectAccountName, KeyIdentifier (master key GUID), RecoveryServer (DC that performed the recovery), RecoveryKeyId (the recovery agent key used). Adversarial use: `mimikatz sekurlsa::dpapi` and `mimikatz dpapi::masterkey /rpc` trigger recovery requests; these generate 4693 events on the source machine. Recovery by an account that did not experience a legitimate password reset or profile loss is suspicious. Recovery of a master key belonging to a high-privilege account (Domain Admin, service account) from an unusual machine or at an unusual time is a high-priority investigation. Also generated by recovery agents (DRA) in PKI environments.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit DPAPI Activity' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > Object Access. Disabled by default.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Credentials from Password Stores: Credentials from Web Browsers

investigationPivots: RecoveryServer identifies which DC processed the recovery — an unexpected DC or a DC in a different site than the user's normal location is anomalous
SubjectAccountName recovering keys for an account that has not recently had a password reset or profile issue is suspicious
Recovery of keys for privileged accounts (Domain Admins, service accounts with broad access) from non-standard workstations is highest priority
Correlate with Sysmon EID 10 (ProcessAccess) targeting lsass.exe in the same timeframe — Mimikatz operations often touch LSASS before or after DPAPI recovery
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate recovery after a user password reset that invalidates the existing master key
Data Recovery Agent (DRA) operations in environments with a configured DPAPI DRA as part of PKI policy
Profile restoration from backup where the master key backup must be recovered from the domain

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account performing master key recovery; unexpected accounts recovering keys may indicate credential harvesting

keyFields.name: MasterKeyId
keyFields.description: GUID of the recovered master key; correlate with 4692 backup events to track the key's history

keyFields.name: RecoveryServer
keyFields.description: DC used for recovery; recovery from an unexpected DC is suspicious

keyFields.name: ReturnCode
keyFields.description: Recovery result code; 0x0 = successful recovery, non-zero = failure

detectionRules.title: DPAPI Master Key Recovery — Suspicious Account or Timing
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4693
| where ReturnCode == "0x0"
| where SubjectUserName != "SYSTEM"
| project TimeGenerated, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, MasterKeyId, RecoveryServer, Computer
detectionRules.notes: Successful DPAPI master key recovery by non-SYSTEM accounts. May indicate Mimikatz 'sekurlsa::dpapi' or manual credential extraction. Correlate with 4692 events for the same MasterKeyId.

detectionRules.title: Successful DPAPI Master Key Recovery by Non-System Account
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4693 Return_Code=0x0 NOT Account_Name=SYSTEM
| table _time, Account_Name, Master_Key_ID, Recovery_Server, host
detectionRules.notes: Successful key recovery by user accounts; correlate with DPAPI backup events (4692).

detectionRules.title: DPAPI Master Key Recovery by Non-System Account
detectionRules.rule:
title: DPAPI Master Key Recovery by Non-System Account
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4693
        ReturnCode: '0x0'
    filter_system:
        SubjectUserName: 'SYSTEM'
    condition: selection and not filter_system
falsepositives:
    - Administrator-initiated credential recovery procedures
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Pair with process creation events to identify the tool performing the recovery.

entries.title: Protection of auditable protected data was attempted
entries.summary: An application attempted to encrypt data using DPAPI with auditing enabled on the data.
entries.details: Generated when CryptProtectData is called on data that was marked as auditable (using the CRYPTPROTECT_AUDIT flag). This is an application-level event — most DPAPI encryption operations do NOT generate this event unless the application explicitly opts into DPAPI auditing by setting the audit flag on the data. Key fields: SubjectAccountName, DataDescription (application-provided label for the protected data), ProcessName (which process called CryptProtectData). Applications that use auditable DPAPI include some credential managers, enterprise password vaults, and security-sensitive applications. In attack contexts, a malicious application masquerading as a legitimate one and calling CryptProtectData with audit flags may generate unexpected 4694 events for processes that should not be encrypting credentials. This event is rare in most environments — any unexpected source is worth investigating.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit DPAPI Activity' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > Object Access. Additionally, the application must call CryptProtectData with the CRYPTPROTECT_AUDIT flag.

investigationPivots: ProcessName that is not a known credential manager or enterprise application encrypting data is anomalous
DataDescription field (set by the calling application) may reveal what data type is being protected — unexpected descriptions from unexpected processes are suspicious
Correlate SubjectAccountName with session activity — encryption by a background process in a logged-off user's context is unusual
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate credential manager applications that explicitly use the CRYPTPROTECT_AUDIT flag for compliance purposes
Enterprise security applications that encrypt sensitive configuration data with auditing enabled

entries.title: Unprotection of auditable protected data was attempted
entries.summary: An application attempted to decrypt DPAPI-protected auditable data.
entries.details: The decryption counterpart to EID 4694 — generated when CryptUnprotectData is called on data that was originally protected with the CRYPTPROTECT_AUDIT flag. Key fields: SubjectAccountName, DataDescription (the label assigned when the data was originally encrypted), ProcessName. This event is more security-relevant than 4694 because decryption is the exfiltration step — an attacker who obtains DPAPI-protected credential blobs and can decrypt them (by recovering the master key first via 4692/4693) may trigger this event if the original encryption used the audit flag. The DataDescription field is particularly valuable: it may directly reveal what credential type is being decrypted (e.g., "Windows Live", "IE/Edge credentials", "RDP password"). ProcessName that is unexpected for the DataDescription type is a key pivot.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit DPAPI Activity' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > Object Access. Additionally, the data must have been originally encrypted with the CRYPTPROTECT_AUDIT flag.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Credentials from Password Stores: Credentials from Web Browsers

investigationPivots: DataDescription containing credential-related strings (browser names, 'RDP', 'password', application names) being decrypted by an unexpected ProcessName is a high-confidence credential theft indicator
ProcessName that is powershell.exe, cmd.exe, or any non-browser/non-credential-manager process decrypting browser or application credentials is critical
SubjectAccountName decrypting credentials belonging to a different user's data (cross-user decryption) indicates lateral credential theft
Correlate with EID 4693 (master key recovery) in the same session — recovery followed immediately by decryption indicates active credential extraction
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate applications decrypting their own credential stores during normal startup or authentication flows
Enterprise credential managers performing routine decryption for password autofill
